Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-SVF3-RY53-C310-00000-00Erin Petrey responds to Herald-Leader's endorsement in KY's 6th District | Opinion2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexisErin Petrey responds to Herald-Leader's endorsement in KY's 6th District | OpinionErin Petrey

KY-06 voters have been given the same type of establishment, corporate Democrat candidates for too long. We need new, relevant voices who won't negotiate against ourselves, not Republican-lite. Incremental change isn't acceptable anymore. We need bold ideas, relentless drive. That's why I, Erin Petrey, a proud, progressive Democrat, am the best option to flip this seat in November.

You always know where I stand. I speak plainly on issues, a stark contrast to the endorsed candidate. I believe in Medicare for All, regardless of who's in charge. I stand against genocide and am endorsed by TrackAIPAC. I'm the only candidate to call to abolish ICE and demand a data center moratorium. I always show up for voters, regardless if they can write a check.

As for experience, I'm not a 3D printed candidate. I'm a real person with a dynamic career building real things, creating jobs, and fighting for sustainability. I have deep experience in renewable energy, international affairs, healthcare, and infrastructure - getting things done in the real world, not just talking points.

If you want an establishment, elitist candidate who quits when things get tough and is funded by out of district billionaires, then I'm not for you. If you want an unapologetic fighter, someone who cannot be bought, and will push the Democratic Party to be better, then I'm asking for your vote on May 19th.

Erin Petrey wrote this piece in response to the Herald-Leader's endorsement of Zach Dembo for the Democratic primary in the U.S. Sixth District House race. The Herald-Leader invites candidates who were not endorsed to respond with an op-ed.

value.Title: Josh Bills: Protect REAP a federal program rural Ky. can't afford to lose
value.Overview: The Daily Independent (Ashland, Kentucky), OPINION, 701words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-3193-RRSH-3196-00000-00Josh Bills: Protect REAP a federal program rural Ky. can't afford to lose2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexisOPINIONJosh Bills: Protect REAP a federal program rural Ky. can't afford to loseStaff Writer

Over the past several decades, rural Kentucky has faced significant changes. In Eastern Kentucky, a shrinking tax base, population loss, and the decline of long-standing industries has made resilience an ever-changing challenge. Through it all, our communities have adapted and searched for new ways to build a stronger, more diverse economy.

Increasingly, a new crisis has emerged: the soaring cost of keeping the lights on. For small businesses and farms that are essential to our local economy, electric rates for commercial facilities have more than doubled over the last 20 years. When you layer in historic inflation, the math becomes overwhelming. Overhead costs eat away at already thin margins, forcing owners to choose between a new hire, a new piece of equipment, or simply paying the utility bill.

At Mountain Association, we have been supporting small businesses since 1976 and saw this energy crisis coming early. In 2008, as costs began their steep climb, we launched an energy savings program. Through this work we continually see one of the biggest challenges facing businesses is upfront capital.

That's where the Rural Energy for America Program REAP, for short comes in. REAP is a federal program administered by the U.S. Department of Agriculture that provides guaranteed loan financing and grant funding to agricultural producers and rural small businesses for renewable energy systems or energy efficiency improvements. For a rural small business owner, that means a grant can cover up to 25 percent of the cost of solar, more efficient HVAC system, LED lighting, or better insulation. These are the kinds of upgrades that pay for themselves in a short time, reducing overhead and improving the bottom line. But for many, the upfront cost is simply out of reach.

At Mountain Association, we have been packaging REAP grants for our clients since 2009. To date, our team has secured more than 60 REAP grants for small businesses and farms across eastern Kentucky, totaling over $2.5 million for our clients. These energy savings projects keep rural businesses open and competitive. As a bonus, many of the projects are completed by local contractors, allowing those federal investments to go even further in supporting eastern Kentucky's economy. Without support from programs like REAP, some of the businesses and farms we work with simply won't make it as costs continue to rise.

Mike Long is the general manager of Long's Pic Pac in Pineville, a town of about 1,630 people in Southeastern Kentucky. His father started the business in 1964 with a $3,500 loan. Today, Mike is fighting to keep it the grocery store of choice for a community where the median household income is just $27,159. Grocery stores typically run on a razor-thin 2.2% profit margin. One bad year or one season of sky-high demand charges and a rural store can vanish.

For Long's Pic Pac, a REAP grant funded 40% of a project to install solar panels on the store's roof and a 60-kW battery. The battery can store excess solar energy and vastly reduce the punishing "demand charges" that make up more than half of the grocery's monthly power bill. The result is an estimated cost savings of at least $15,000 per yearmoney that can go to staffing the deli, offsetting delivery costs, or simply keeping prices stable for families in Pineville. Long expects to pay back the entire cost of the project in just four years.

The need for this program has never been more urgent. With each devastating flood or winter storm, utility companies are forced to make expensive repairs to aging infrastructure. Businesses and ratepayers pay for those repairs through higher rates. As the frequency and intensity of these storms increase, utilities will continue passing those recovery costs back to us. For a business like Long's Pic Pac living on a 2.2% margin, this compounding cycle of damage, recovery, and rate hikes is a threat to its existence.

REAP is a proven, efficient tool that uses modest federal investment to unlock private capital and lower operating costs. But it only works if it is funded and protected. It isn't just an energy policy. It is the difference between closing the doors and keeping the lights on.

value.Title: Data center regulations weighed; some worry over jobs, energy, taxes
value.Overview: The Center Square, RENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS, 589words

urn:contentItem:6JK4-M4G3-RRYS-M1GN-00000-00Data center regulations weighed; some worry over jobs, energy, taxes2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:14ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWSData center regulations weighed; some worry over jobs, energy, taxesBy Sean Reed | The Center Square

(The Center Square) - Major bills in both the state Senate and House may heavily regulate data centers in the state. With the proposal spanning hundreds of pages, both those in favor and against the bill have a lot of arguments.

With a little more than two weeks of legislative session remaining, lawmakers have continued to weigh if and how they might regulate data centers over energy consumption and related issues.

A House Committee heard from multiple environmental organizations in favor of the 632-page bill on data centers late Tuesday.

House Bill 5513 would expand what permits data centers will be required to obtain, require them to report their energy use, pay into community funds and plan to save water and while bringing new energy capacity to the grid, according to Rep. Carol Ammons, D-Urbana.

Andrew Rehn and Tyshianna Bankhead, both representing member organizations of the Illinois Clean Jobs Coalition, and Brian McDaniel from Citizens Utility Board all spoke in favor of the plan.

Rehn said water consumption by data centers could leave Illinoisans without vital resources.

"Illinois lacks meaningful protections against large water users like data centers overusing our water and unlike many of our neighboring states, we do not regulate or meaningfully monitor water usage or overconsumption," Rehn said.

McDaniel favors the act because it will force new data center projects to bring renewable energy projects alongside development, expanding grid capacity as usage increases.

"Large data centers can increase these costs for all of us on different levels of the power system, from the utility level, the distribution level, to the high-voltage transmission lines, all the way to the markets that set our prices for power generation," McDaniel said.

Questions arose about data center power usage, along with associated rate hikes.

State Rep. Blaine Wilhour, R-Louisville, noted that recent rate hikes being associated with data centers did not happen in a vacuum.

"You've got to take that into the context of the capacity that had been previously taken off the market because of legislation that this side of the table supported," Wilhour said, referring to Democrats' support of the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act.

Brad Tietz from the Data Center Coalition and Joe Duffy from Climate Jobs Illinois were against the legislation, though for varying reasons.

Duffy, representing trade unions and other members of the renewable energy industry, said those he represents oppose the bill because the bill doesn't strongly enough regulate how the required energy projects will be built.

"We have concerns about several provisions," Duffy said. "A data center developer could meet Illinois' clean energy obligations by building a non-union solar project in Ohio, Missouri, or Indiana, for example, then count it toward their Illinois requirements, collect Illinois incentives, and generate none of the benefits here."

Tietz, however, opposes the bill on behalf of data center interests. He said new projects will be highly discouraged from choosing Illinois as the place to build, pushing jobs to neighboring states.

"Every data center is different. They have different purposes, different workloads, and activities occurring in them and different customers," Tietz said.

Tietz went on to say the bill is restrictive on all centers, even though they are not all created equal.

Tietz also said new data center projects have vastly contributed to the state's tax base and economy in recent years, providing jobs in various sectors statewide.

value.Title: AKFA expanding in Bowling Green with 331 jobs
value.Overview: The Messenger (Madisonville, Kentucky), NEWS, 577words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ2-PH03-RS2G-81PM-00000-00AKFA expanding in Bowling Green with 331 jobs2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexisNEWSAKFA expanding in Bowling Green with 331 jobsThe Messenger Staff

FRANKFORT, Ky. Gov. Andy Beshear congratulated AKFA Aluminum Solutions Thursday as the company began work on a new manufacturing facility in Bowling Green, according to a release.

AKFA, a division of AKFA Group, is the first Uzbekistan-based company to establish a manufacturing operation in the United States. The project is expected to create 331 jobs in Kentucky.

"Today's event is a great reminder that Kentucky's economic momentum remains strong as the commonwealth welcomes companies from all over the world to their New Kentucky Home," Beshear said. "This project is bringing great new job opportunities for Kentuckians in Bowling Green and the surrounding region."

The new facility will serve as a full-cycle production hub with extrusion, anodizing and finishing capabilities that meet U.S. energy and environmental standards. Company officials said the operation will use recycled aluminum billets and energy-efficient systems to support a low-carbon supply chain, the release said.

A public celebration was held Monday at the Team Kentucky booth during the SelectUSA Investment Summit in National Harbor. Officials from AKFA and the Cabinet for Economic Development marked the formal launch of the company's presence in Kentucky.

Alisher Srajidinov, management board member and chief operating officer of AKFA Aluminum Group, said the project marks an important step in the company's long-term presence in the United States and will help the company better serve customers while bringing advanced manufacturing capabilities to south-central Kentucky.

AKFA Aluminum Solutions U.S. LLC operates the aluminum division of AKFA Group, one of Central Asia's largest industrial companies. The company employs more than 8,000 people across 20 facilities and produces up to 100,000 tons of product annually for construction, transportation and renewable energy markets.

Doug Gorman, judge-executive of Warren County, said the investment will create new opportunities for residents, the workforce and local businesses.

Todd Alcott said the project aligns with Bowling Green's long-term growth strategy and strengthens the city's ability to attract international investment.

Meredith Wilson said AKFA's decision to locate its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Bowling Green reflects the region's strength as a destination for international investment.

The project adds to what state officials describe as Kentucky's strongest six-year period of economic growth. Since the beginning of Beshear's administration, more than 1,300 private-sector new-location and expansion projects totaling more than $50 billion in announced investment have been announced, with more than 70,000 jobs expected, according to the release.

In December 2025, the Kentucky Economic Development Finance Authority preliminarily approved a 10-year incentive agreement for the company through the Kentucky Business Investment program. The performance-based agreement allows tax incentives tied to investment and annual job targets.

The authority also approved tax incentives through the Kentucky Enterprise Initiative Act, which allows qualifying companies to recover Kentucky sales and use tax on construction costs, building fixtures, research and development equipment and electronic processing equipment.

AKFA will also be eligible for workforce support services, including recruitment assistance, job placement services, customized training and job-training incentives, the release said.

AKFA, a division of AKFA Group, is the first Uzbekistan-based company to establish a manufacturing operation in the United States. The project is expected to create 331 jobs in Kentucky.

"Today's event is a great reminder that Kentucky's economic momentum remains strong as the commonwealth welcomes companies from all over the world to their New Kentucky Home," Beshear said in a news release from the state. "This project is bringing great new job opportunities for Kentuckians in Bowling Green and the surrounding region."

The new facility will serve as a full-cycle production hub with extrusion, anodizing and finishing capabilities that meet U.S. energy and environmental standards. Company officials said the operation will use recycled aluminum billets and energy-efficient systems to support a low-carbon supply chain, the release said.

A public celebration was held Monday at the Team Kentucky booth during the SelectUSA Investment Summit in National Harbor. Officials from AKFA and the Cabinet for Economic Development marked the formal launch of the company's presence in Kentucky.

Alisher Srajidinov, management board member and chief operating officer of AKFA Aluminum Group, said the project marks an important step in the company's long-term presence in the United States and will help the company better serve customers while bringing advanced manufacturing capabilities to south-central Kentucky.

AKFA Aluminum Solutions U.S. LLC operates the aluminum division of AKFA Group, one of Central Asia's largest industrial companies. The company employs more than 8,000 people across 20 facilities and produces up to 100,000 tons of product annually for construction, transportation and renewable energy markets.

Doug Gorman, judge-executive of Warren County, said the investment will create new opportunities for residents, the workforce and local businesses.

Todd Alcott said the project aligns with Bowling Green's long-term growth strategy and strengthens the city's ability to attract international investment.

Meredith Wilson said AKFA's decision to locate its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Bowling Green reflects the region's strength as a destination for international investment.

The project adds to what state officials describe as Kentucky's strongest six-year period of economic growth. Since the beginning of Beshear's administration, more than 1,300 private-sector new-location and expansion projects totaling more than $50 billion in announced investment have been announced, with more than 70,000 jobs expected, according to the release.

In December 2025, the Kentucky Economic Development Finance Authority preliminarily approved a 10-year incentive agreement for the company through the Kentucky Business Investment program. The performance-based agreement allows tax incentives tied to investment and annual job targets.

The authority also approved tax incentives through the Kentucky Enterprise Initiative Act, which allows qualifying companies to recover Kentucky sales and use tax on construction costs, building fixtures, research and development equipment and electronic processing equipment.

AKFA will also be eligible for workforce support services, including recruitment assistance, job placement services, customized training and job-training incentives, the release said.

value.Title: St. Regis Residences secures $114 million loan from local bank South Florida's news briefs for the week of May 8
value.Overview: South Florida Business Journal (Fort Lauderdale/Miami, Florida), 552words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-9473-RYTS-72V1-00000-00St. Regis Residences secures $114 million loan from local bank2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:45:58ZLexisNexisSt. Regis Residences secures $114 million loan from local bankSouth Florida's news briefs for the week of May 8Eileen Cukier

Owners Lombardi Properties and Fox Farm Capital announced the completion of The Doris, an eight-story boutique office property at 153 N.W. 25th St., in Miami. www.lombardiproperties.com.

PMG launched sales for One Twenty Brickell Signature Residences, a 40-story mixed-use tower offering a deeded office suite with each of its 467 fully furnished residences, at 120 S.W. Eighth St. in Miami. www.onetwentyresidences.com.

Blue Oceans Yachting relocated its Fort Lauderdale office to 1635 Miami Road, Suite 3. www.blueoceansyachting.com.

McDowell Housing Partners completed Miami Jewish Health's Douglas Gardens Senior Health and Living, a 410-unit mixed-income, mixed-use residential community at 711 and 713 S.W. 88th Ave. in Pembroke Pines. www.douglasgardensapts.com.

Broward Health broke ground on an eight-story, 188,000-square-foot medical office building and parking garage at Broward Health Medical Center, 1600 S. Andrews Ave. in Fort Lauderdale. www.browardhealth.org.

Miami-based I Squared Capital's Energy Transition Fund acquired Oriden, a Pittsburgh-based renewable energy developer, from Mitsubishi Power Americas. www.isquaredcapital.com.

Ed Morse Automotive Group acquired Graven Chrysler Jeep Ram in Lebanon, Missouri. www.edmorse.com.

Miami-based Consertus acquired Laceco, a Beirut-based architecture, engineering and planning consultancy. www.consertus.com.

Lexington, Kentucky-based managed cybersecurity services provider 13 Layers opened an office at Crown Center Executive Suites, 1451 W. Cypress Creek Road, Suite 300, in Fort Lauderdale. www.13layers.com.

Fortune International Group and Château Group closed on a $113.75 million land loan with City National Bank of Florida for the North Tower at The St. Regis Residences, Sunny Isles Beach. The tower is on track to break ground at 18801 Collins Ave. this summer. www.srresidencessunnyislesbeach.com.

The Lynd Group secured $34.5 million in refinancing from A10 Capital for Parc Place Apartments, a multifamily community at 17600 N.W. Fifth Ave. in Miami Gardens. www.lynd.com.

Berkadia secured a $21.58 million loan on behalf of Rental Asset Management to refinance South Pointe Apartments, a 170-unit apartment community at 17800 S.W. 107th Ave. in the West Perrine area of Miami-Dade County. www.berkadia.com.

Florida Community Loan Fund closed on $11 million in New Markets Tax Credit financing for Boys & Girls Clubs of Palm Beach County's Julie & Mike Connors Club, a new 31,000-square-foot youth development and workforce readiness clubhouse on 1.65 acres at 1150 Southern Blvd. in West Palm Beach. www.fclf.org.

Berger Commercial Realty facilitated Sand Lake Realty's $6.325 million purchase of the small-bay industrial property at 1241-1251 N Dixie Highway in Pompano Beach, which it will rebrand as Airpark Commerce Center. www.bergercommercial.com.

To be considered for inclusion, please email news releases and high-resolution photos to newstipssfbj@bizjournals.com

Sign up here for the South Florida Business Journal's free morning and afternoon daily newsletters to receive the latest business news impacting Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties. Download the free Business Journal app for breaking news alerts on your phone.

value.Title: May 11, 2026: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO., INC. PEER LANDSCAPE: RECENT NEWS HIGHLIGHTS
value.Overview: News Bites - Private Companies, ANNOUNCEMENTS, 610words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJM-G8H3-S3TD-1210-00000-00May 11, 2026: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO., INC. PEER LANDSCAPE: RECENT NEWS HIGHLIGHTS2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 11, 2026: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO., INC. PEER LANDSCAPE: RECENT NEWS HIGHLIGHTS

On February 25, 2026, Consigli Construction Co., Inc. commenced work on the Williams College Museum of Art, marking the first purpose-built facility for the museum on the campus. Abigail Berkebile, a prominent figure at the firm, noted the unique design challenges and the rewarding nature of witnessing staff enthusiasm as the building progresses. She also emphasized the diverse career opportunities available for women in the construction industry, noting that teams rely on expertise in accounting, IT, legal, and other fields that do not require a formal construction background. To maintain focus during complex projects, Berkebile highlighted her routine of organizing schedules and connecting with her team daily to foster collaboration.

In the broader competitive landscape, Suffolk Construction is advancing its regional infrastructure commitments. The firm managed the Southeastern Public Service Authority interchange project that led to the Virginia Department of Transportation reopening a long-term lane closure on U.S. Route 58 on May 5, 2026. The completion of this project facilitates a new ramp and flyover for eastbound traffic to access the regional landfill without crossing westbound lanes. Meanwhile, a solar farm in Suffolk currently under construction by Egg Power, set to commence generation in 2027, became the center of a 10-year Power Purchase Agreement announced on May 1, 2026. Virgin Media O2 will source approximately five percent of its total energy demands from this 70MW facility. Mark Hardman, Director of Finance Operations at VMO2, stated that the agreement represents the latest step in securing long-term renewable energy and achieving net-zero emissions by the end of 2040.

Expanding on significant industry developments, Turner Construction celebrated the topping out of the 72-megawatt Aligned IAD-06 Data Center in Frederick, Maryland, on April 28, 2026. The 450,000-square-foot facility, located on a 75-acre campus, logged over 80,000 craft hours with approximately 345 skilled workers. Nick Sevier, Operations Manager at Turner, remarked that the milestone reflects the strength of the partnership with Aligned and the dedication to delivering a complex data center safely. On that same day, Turner Construction commenced vertical construction on a $900 million expansion of the Geisinger Wyoming Valley Medical Center in Pennsylvania. The 11-story, 600,000-square-foot patient tower will increase the hospital's capacity to 500 beds and is slated to open in early 2028. David Kaminski, Vice President and Senior General Manager at Turner, stated that the project will enhance patient care and support caregivers while generating a significant local economic impact.

Shaping its financial strategies to remain competitive with peers, Gilbane Development appointed Andreas Vlahakis as head of capital markets on April 28, 2026. Operating from New York City and reporting to President and CEO James Patchett, Vlahakis oversees funding strategies, investment decisions, and equity partnerships for ground-up multifamily and student housing investments. In a related push for student housing development, the Western Kentucky University Student Government Association confirmed a public-private partnership with Gilbane following legislative approval on April 6, 2026, and initial authorization on December 11, 2025. The firm will design and develop new residence halls to replace outdated facilities on campus. SGA Senator Jakob Barker confirmed the authorization, noting that the university is now cleared to begin building the new accommodations.

value.Title: House Hearing Frames Clean Energy , Cost Debate Ahead Of Midterms
value.Overview: Superfund Report, 1206words

urn:contentItem:6JJF-JXP3-SFGN-K384-00000-00House Hearing Frames Clean Energy, Cost Debate Ahead Of Midterms2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexisHouse Hearing Frames Clean Energy, Cost Debate Ahead Of Midterms

April 30, 2026 As House Republicans pitch a series of bills intended to address consumer concerns about rising energy prices, a recent committee hearing framed the partisan debate over how to meet data centers' electricity demands, with Republicans blaming strict environmental requirements and Democrats pushing for cheaper, clean energy sources.

The April 29 House Energy & Commerce energy panel hearing also offered a snapshot of the broader debate over how to expedite major infrastructure projects, with Republicans arguing strict environmental reviews and permitting requirements are hampering such efforts while Democrats asserted grid upgrades would accelerate wind and solar energy.

Broadly, the debate could help determine whether, and how, responding to increased power demand should reshape permitting and other environmental regulations -- with the Trump administration arguing artificial intelligence (AI) development is a top priority requiring streamlined environmental rules.

"Last year, Republicans used reconciliation to wreck our power grid and repeal billions of tax incentives and programs that would have financed hundreds of gigawatts of clean, reliable power," full committee ranking member Frank Pallone (D-NJ) said at the hearing.

"I fundamentally find it difficult to trust proposals to fix our problems from the same people who caused those problems in the first place," Pallone added.

The seven bills being considered by the subcommittee include a proposal known as the Fair Allocation of Interstate Rates (FAIR) Act that would exclude the costs associated with states' clean energy standards from utility rates charged to consumers, which Democrats blasted as a veiled attempt at punishing renewable energy policies.

Republicans are also backing a plan to codify President Trump's voluntary ratepayer "protection pledge" in which large technology companies pledged to pay for grid upgrades to accommodate the growth of data centers and AI.

Democrats and environmentalists say the pledge lacks an enforcement mechanism for ensuring tech companies live up to their promise. Instead, Democrats at the hearing pushed a proposal requiring the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission to develop a strategy for protecting consumers from large sources of power demand such as data centers, based on an analysis that Democrats expect will show that renewables offer the cleanest, cheapest and quickest options to expand generation.

Despite the sparring, full committee Chairman Brett Guthrie (R-KY) claimed the bills offer a starting point for bipartisan negotiations on revising environmental and other requirements to expedite approval of large infrastructure projects.

"So, while we may not agree with every provision in the bills, these proposals will foster discussion about how to implement a vision of our electric system that keeps costs low for households and ensures U.S. leadership in the next generation economy," Guthrie told Democrats.

'Small Steps'

Pallone agreed that some of the measures floated at the hearing should spark further discussion.

"While I think some of these bills need tweaks or technical alterations, they're largely small steps in the right direction," Pallone said. "But we don't need small steps. We need big steps because we have big problems."

He said the hearing's omission of a Democratic bill on grid reliability was "a real missed opportunity, and I'm disappointed that it was not included."

The House in December approved the SPEED Act, which would streamline environmental permitting by revising core provisions of the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). The bill, H.R. 4776, was approved largely by a party-line vote, but the Senate has been struggling for months to craft its own permitting agreement.

Generally, Republicans argue large demand sources like data centers will save average consumers money by paying for grid upgrades. But Democrats say we should not undercut progress on clean air and water to accelerate the growth of data centers. Instead, preserving strict environmental standards will guarantee they rely on the cleanest energy sources.

The partisan chasm was also on display during a Senate hearing the same day, where Senate energy committee ranking member Martin Heinrich (D-NM) accused Interior Secretary Doug Burgum of obstructing renewable energy and threatening any hope for a bipartisan permitting deal.

"I'll just be very blunt," Heinrich said at the April 29 hearing. "If permitting reform is a priority, the permitting system has to work. And if, if you want members of my caucus to support permitting reform, we have to know that balls and strikes are going to be called, that there is not a thumb on the scale of this system. That confidence does not exist right now."

Heinrich made the comments while querying Burgum about whether the Interior Department (DOI) would comply with an April 21 court order barring DOI from implementing five policies that target renewables projects for additional layers of review.

While Burgum said active litigation is under the Justice Department's purview, he added: "We disagree vehemently with the judge's view that somehow having appropriate reviews of energy projects on our internal procedures is" subject to judicial review.

Heinrich also argued that the Trump administration's fiscal year 2027 budget request for DOI "tells me that this administration isn't serious about permitting reform, because defunding the agencies that issue permits for new energy projects means that permits don't get issued, creating red tape, rather than cutting it."

EPA Agenda

Meanwhile, EPA Administrator Zeldin told an April 28 House Energy and Commerce subcommittee hearing that the White House has generated recommendations on permitting under the Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, Endangered Species Act, and National Environmental Policy Act, with EPA having "additional ideas on top of that."

Zeldin added: "I believe that we have provided that list, we can provide that again, to any members of your caucus who are asking for it and involved in this process. There are many ways that you can modernize these landmark laws that have been on the books for a long time, to have a permitting process that takes less time, costs less money and has more certainty."

At the same time, Zeldin pledged to soon issue new guidance easing industry compliance with Biden-era oil and gas methane standards, which were "used to cause a lot of harm to much of this country."

The flurry of Hill activity came amid a first-time international meeting April 24-29 in Colombia discussing how to transition away from fossil fuels. The conference was hosted by the Netherlands and Colombia to guide countries on practical steps for adopting clean energy.

The Trump administration did not attend, saying that "moving away from reliable, affordable, and secure energy to rely on intermittent and costly energy sources is destructive," according to a State Department statement. It described the gathering of leaders from about 60 countries as part of a "bogus climate agenda."

China, the world's largest consumer of coal, also did not attend, underscoring broader international divisions on climate and clean energy efforts. -- Rick Weber (rweber@iwpnews.com)

value.Title: House Hearing Frames Clean Energy , Cost Debate Ahead Of Midterms
value.Overview: EnergyWashington Week, 1206words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-H973-RX3H-30J0-00000-00House Hearing Frames Clean Energy, Cost Debate Ahead Of Midterms2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexisHouse Hearing Frames Clean Energy, Cost Debate Ahead Of Midterms

April 30, 2026 As House Republicans pitch a series of bills intended to address consumer concerns about rising energy prices, a recent committee hearing framed the partisan debate over how to meet data centers' electricity demands, with Republicans blaming strict environmental requirements and Democrats pushing for cheaper, clean energy sources.

The April 29 House Energy & Commerce energy panel hearing also offered a snapshot of the broader debate over how to expedite major infrastructure projects, with Republicans arguing strict environmental reviews and permitting requirements are hampering such efforts while Democrats asserted grid upgrades would accelerate wind and solar energy.

Broadly, the debate could help determine whether, and how, responding to increased power demand should reshape permitting and other environmental regulations -- with the Trump administration arguing artificial intelligence (AI) development is a top priority requiring streamlined environmental rules.

"Last year, Republicans used reconciliation to wreck our power grid and repeal billions of tax incentives and programs that would have financed hundreds of gigawatts of clean, reliable power," full committee ranking member Frank Pallone (D-NJ) said at the hearing.

"I fundamentally find it difficult to trust proposals to fix our problems from the same people who caused those problems in the first place," Pallone added.

The seven bills being considered by the subcommittee include a proposal known as the Fair Allocation of Interstate Rates (FAIR) Act that would exclude the costs associated with states' clean energy standards from utility rates charged to consumers, which Democrats blasted as a veiled attempt at punishing renewable energy policies.

Republicans are also backing a plan to codify President Trump's voluntary ratepayer "protection pledge" in which large technology companies pledged to pay for grid upgrades to accommodate the growth of data centers and AI.

Democrats and environmentalists say the pledge lacks an enforcement mechanism for ensuring tech companies live up to their promise. Instead, Democrats at the hearing pushed a proposal requiring the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission to develop a strategy for protecting consumers from large sources of power demand such as data centers, based on an analysis that Democrats expect will show that renewables offer the cleanest, cheapest and quickest options to expand generation.

Despite the sparring, full committee Chairman Brett Guthrie (R-KY) claimed the bills offer a starting point for bipartisan negotiations on revising environmental and other requirements to expedite approval of large infrastructure projects.

"So, while we may not agree with every provision in the bills, these proposals will foster discussion about how to implement a vision of our electric system that keeps costs low for households and ensures U.S. leadership in the next generation economy," Guthrie told Democrats.

'Small Steps'

Pallone agreed that some of the measures floated at the hearing should spark further discussion.

"While I think some of these bills need tweaks or technical alterations, they're largely small steps in the right direction," Pallone said. "But we don't need small steps. We need big steps because we have big problems."

He said the hearing's omission of a Democratic bill on grid reliability was "a real missed opportunity, and I'm disappointed that it was not included."

The House in December approved the SPEED Act, which would streamline environmental permitting by revising core provisions of the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). The bill, H.R. 4776, was approved largely by a party-line vote, but the Senate has been struggling for months to craft its own permitting agreement.

Generally, Republicans argue large demand sources like data centers will save average consumers money by paying for grid upgrades. But Democrats say we should not undercut progress on clean air and water to accelerate the growth of data centers. Instead, preserving strict environmental standards will guarantee they rely on the cleanest energy sources.

The partisan chasm was also on display during a Senate hearing the same day, where Senate energy committee ranking member Martin Heinrich (D-NM) accused Interior Secretary Doug Burgum of obstructing renewable energy and threatening any hope for a bipartisan permitting deal.

"I'll just be very blunt," Heinrich said at the April 29 hearing. "If permitting reform is a priority, the permitting system has to work. And if, if you want members of my caucus to support permitting reform, we have to know that balls and strikes are going to be called, that there is not a thumb on the scale of this system. That confidence does not exist right now."

Heinrich made the comments while querying Burgum about whether the Interior Department (DOI) would comply with an April 21 court order barring DOI from implementing five policies that target renewables projects for additional layers of review.

While Burgum said active litigation is under the Justice Department's purview, he added: "We disagree vehemently with the judge's view that somehow having appropriate reviews of energy projects on our internal procedures is" subject to judicial review.

Heinrich also argued that the Trump administration's fiscal year 2027 budget request for DOI "tells me that this administration isn't serious about permitting reform, because defunding the agencies that issue permits for new energy projects means that permits don't get issued, creating red tape, rather than cutting it."

EPA Agenda

Meanwhile, EPA Administrator Zeldin told an April 28 House Energy and Commerce subcommittee hearing that the White House has generated recommendations on permitting under the Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, Endangered Species Act, and National Environmental Policy Act, with EPA having "additional ideas on top of that."

Zeldin added: "I believe that we have provided that list, we can provide that again, to any members of your caucus who are asking for it and involved in this process. There are many ways that you can modernize these landmark laws that have been on the books for a long time, to have a permitting process that takes less time, costs less money and has more certainty."

At the same time, Zeldin pledged to soon issue new guidance easing industry compliance with Biden-era oil and gas methane standards, which were "used to cause a lot of harm to much of this country."

The flurry of Hill activity came amid a first-time international meeting April 24-29 in Colombia discussing how to transition away from fossil fuels. The conference was hosted by the Netherlands and Colombia to guide countries on practical steps for adopting clean energy.

The Trump administration did not attend, saying that "moving away from reliable, affordable, and secure energy to rely on intermittent and costly energy sources is destructive," according to a State Department statement. It described the gathering of leaders from about 60 countries as part of a "bogus climate agenda."

China, the world's largest consumer of coal, also did not attend, underscoring broader international divisions on climate and clean energy efforts. -- Rick Weber (rweber@iwpnews.com)

urn:contentItem:6JHC-YV13-SHN4-41HG-00000-00How Far Can Duke Energy Corporation (NYSE:DUK), Push Its Gains2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:46:15ZLexisNexisHow Far Can Duke Energy Corporation (NYSE:DUK), Push Its Gains

NYSE STOCK REPORT

CHARLOTTE, Tuesday, May 05 2026 - Ahead of this morning's market opening, Duke Energy Corporation closed higher the previous trading day. Duke Energy Corporation (NYSE:DUK), the NYSE's largest Utility company by market cap, increased 0.1% during its previous trading session to close at $127.63.

SECTION 2 TODAY'S BULLISH SIGNALS

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

value.Title: House Hearing Frames Clean Energy , Cost Debate Ahead Of Midterms
value.Overview: Defense Environment Alert, 1206words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-H973-RX3H-30HR-00000-00House Hearing Frames Clean Energy, Cost Debate Ahead Of Midterms2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexisHouse Hearing Frames Clean Energy, Cost Debate Ahead Of Midterms

April 30, 2026 As House Republicans pitch a series of bills intended to address consumer concerns about rising energy prices, a recent committee hearing framed the partisan debate over how to meet data centers' electricity demands, with Republicans blaming strict environmental requirements and Democrats pushing for cheaper, clean energy sources.

The April 29 House Energy & Commerce energy panel hearing also offered a snapshot of the broader debate over how to expedite major infrastructure projects, with Republicans arguing strict environmental reviews and permitting requirements are hampering such efforts while Democrats asserted grid upgrades would accelerate wind and solar energy.

Broadly, the debate could help determine whether, and how, responding to increased power demand should reshape permitting and other environmental regulations -- with the Trump administration arguing artificial intelligence (AI) development is a top priority requiring streamlined environmental rules.

"Last year, Republicans used reconciliation to wreck our power grid and repeal billions of tax incentives and programs that would have financed hundreds of gigawatts of clean, reliable power," full committee ranking member Frank Pallone (D-NJ) said at the hearing.

"I fundamentally find it difficult to trust proposals to fix our problems from the same people who caused those problems in the first place," Pallone added.

The seven bills being considered by the subcommittee include a proposal known as the Fair Allocation of Interstate Rates (FAIR) Act that would exclude the costs associated with states' clean energy standards from utility rates charged to consumers, which Democrats blasted as a veiled attempt at punishing renewable energy policies.

Republicans are also backing a plan to codify President Trump's voluntary ratepayer "protection pledge" in which large technology companies pledged to pay for grid upgrades to accommodate the growth of data centers and AI.

Democrats and environmentalists say the pledge lacks an enforcement mechanism for ensuring tech companies live up to their promise. Instead, Democrats at the hearing pushed a proposal requiring the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission to develop a strategy for protecting consumers from large sources of power demand such as data centers, based on an analysis that Democrats expect will show that renewables offer the cleanest, cheapest and quickest options to expand generation.

Despite the sparring, full committee Chairman Brett Guthrie (R-KY) claimed the bills offer a starting point for bipartisan negotiations on revising environmental and other requirements to expedite approval of large infrastructure projects.

"So, while we may not agree with every provision in the bills, these proposals will foster discussion about how to implement a vision of our electric system that keeps costs low for households and ensures U.S. leadership in the next generation economy," Guthrie told Democrats.

'Small Steps'

Pallone agreed that some of the measures floated at the hearing should spark further discussion.

"While I think some of these bills need tweaks or technical alterations, they're largely small steps in the right direction," Pallone said. "But we don't need small steps. We need big steps because we have big problems."

He said the hearing's omission of a Democratic bill on grid reliability was "a real missed opportunity, and I'm disappointed that it was not included."

The House in December approved the SPEED Act, which would streamline environmental permitting by revising core provisions of the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). The bill, H.R. 4776, was approved largely by a party-line vote, but the Senate has been struggling for months to craft its own permitting agreement.

Generally, Republicans argue large demand sources like data centers will save average consumers money by paying for grid upgrades. But Democrats say we should not undercut progress on clean air and water to accelerate the growth of data centers. Instead, preserving strict environmental standards will guarantee they rely on the cleanest energy sources.

The partisan chasm was also on display during a Senate hearing the same day, where Senate energy committee ranking member Martin Heinrich (D-NM) accused Interior Secretary Doug Burgum of obstructing renewable energy and threatening any hope for a bipartisan permitting deal.

"I'll just be very blunt," Heinrich said at the April 29 hearing. "If permitting reform is a priority, the permitting system has to work. And if, if you want members of my caucus to support permitting reform, we have to know that balls and strikes are going to be called, that there is not a thumb on the scale of this system. That confidence does not exist right now."

Heinrich made the comments while querying Burgum about whether the Interior Department (DOI) would comply with an April 21 court order barring DOI from implementing five policies that target renewables projects for additional layers of review.

While Burgum said active litigation is under the Justice Department's purview, he added: "We disagree vehemently with the judge's view that somehow having appropriate reviews of energy projects on our internal procedures is" subject to judicial review.

Heinrich also argued that the Trump administration's fiscal year 2027 budget request for DOI "tells me that this administration isn't serious about permitting reform, because defunding the agencies that issue permits for new energy projects means that permits don't get issued, creating red tape, rather than cutting it."

EPA Agenda

Meanwhile, EPA Administrator Zeldin told an April 28 House Energy and Commerce subcommittee hearing that the White House has generated recommendations on permitting under the Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, Endangered Species Act, and National Environmental Policy Act, with EPA having "additional ideas on top of that."

Zeldin added: "I believe that we have provided that list, we can provide that again, to any members of your caucus who are asking for it and involved in this process. There are many ways that you can modernize these landmark laws that have been on the books for a long time, to have a permitting process that takes less time, costs less money and has more certainty."

At the same time, Zeldin pledged to soon issue new guidance easing industry compliance with Biden-era oil and gas methane standards, which were "used to cause a lot of harm to much of this country."

The flurry of Hill activity came amid a first-time international meeting April 24-29 in Colombia discussing how to transition away from fossil fuels. The conference was hosted by the Netherlands and Colombia to guide countries on practical steps for adopting clean energy.

The Trump administration did not attend, saying that "moving away from reliable, affordable, and secure energy to rely on intermittent and costly energy sources is destructive," according to a State Department statement. It described the gathering of leaders from about 60 countries as part of a "bogus climate agenda."

China, the world's largest consumer of coal, also did not attend, underscoring broader international divisions on climate and clean energy efforts. -- Rick Weber (rweber@iwpnews.com)

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

SECTION 2 TODAY'S BULLISH SIGNALS

2.1 Uptrend

Today's Volatility:

The 4% discount to 12-month high of $134.49 against the 20% premium to 12-month low of $107.42 suggests the stock continues to push for new highs.

Today its volatility (highest price minus lowest price/lowest price) of 1.6% equalled the average daily volatility, up from 1.2584091808468567% on Wednesday. Price/Moving Average Price of 1.05:

- The Price/MAP 200 for Duke Energy Corporation is 1.05. Being higher than 1 is a bullish indicator. The stock is trading above both its MAPs and the 50-day MAP of $133.03 is higher than the 200-day MAP of $122.48, a second bullish indicator. The 200-day MAP has increased to $122.48, a third bullish indicator.

Past Quarter:

- In the last three months the stock has hit a new 52-week high twelve times, pointing to a significant uptrend.

2.2 Other Bullish Signals

- Return on Equity of 9.0% versus sector average of 5.8%.

MCap/Total Assets:

- Tobin's Q Ratio, defined as MCap divided by Total Assets, is 0.5. Compared with the rest of the market the stock is undervalued.

- Net profit margin has averaged 14.4% in the last 3 years. This is better than the sector average of 11.8% and suggests a high margin of safety.

- As per the Du Pont analysis, Return on Equity of 9.0% is better than sector average of 5.8%. This is computed as net profit margin of 15.2% times asset turnover [sales/assets] of 0.16 times leverage factor [total assets/shareholders' equity] of 3.6.

SECTION 3 ONGOING BULLISH PARAMETERS

3.1 Rule of 40

The stock scores a favorable score exceeding 40 when using the Rule of 40 (Revenue Growth plus EBITDA margin). Y.o.y revenue growth of 4%, EBITDA margin is 36.2% and the sum of the two 40.7% needs to exceed 40%.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.2 Created Market Value [CMV] past 17 yrs of $79.3 billion

- Market Capitalization has increased by $81.3 billion from $18.6 billion to $99.9 billion in the last 17 years. This increase comprises cumulative retained earnings (RETE) of $2 billion and Created Market Value of $79.3 billion. The Created Market Value multiple, defined by the change in MCap for every $1 of retained earnings is exemplary at $39.99.

3.3 Satisfies four criteria of Benjamin Graham

- The P/E of 22.5 is 0.4 times the highest average P/E of 57.7 in the last five years. This is a value criterion, according to Benjamin Graham who described as a value criterion "A P/E ratio down to less than four-tenth of the highest average P/E ratio the stock attained in the most recent five years".

-""Earnings growth over the most recent ten years of 7% compounded - that is a doubling of earnings in a five-year period""; the earnings per share have more than doubled in the last 5 years.

-""Stability of growth in earnings over the last 5 years, defined as no more than two declines of 5% or greater in year-end earnings""; there have been 2 declines in earnings in the last 5 years.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.4 Satisfies 5 out of 9 criteria of Joseph Piotroski [pass mark 5]:

- Positive net income.

- Positive operating cashflow.

- Return on Assets improvement [from 2.4% to 2.5%].

- Good quality of earnings [operating cashflow exceeds net income].

- Improvement in gross margin [from 98.0% to 98.1%].

But does not meet the following 4 criteria of Joseph Piotroski:

- Improvement in long-term debt to total assets.

- Improvement in current ratio.

- Total shares on issue unchanged (or reduction in total shares on issue).

- Improvement in asset turnover.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

SECTION 4 TODAY'S BEARISH SIGNALS

4.1 Other Bearish Signals

- Total Liabilities/EBITDA of 12.3 is more than 5, this compares unfavourably with the Joseph Piotroski benchmark of 5.

SECTION 5 PEER COMPARISON & RANKING OF DUK

5.1 LONG-TERM FUNDAMENTAL RANKING: 3 OUT OF 5 [5 is best]

Duke Energy Corporation is ranked number 1 out of 32 listed utility companies in the United States with a market capitalization of $99.9 billion.

Within its sector it has a relatively low P/E/G of 0.37.

It has a moderate relative ROE of 9.0% and ROA of 2.5%. The company paid a dividend of $3.19 in the last twelve months. The dividend yield is moderate at 3.3%. Finally, its earnings growth in the past 12 months has been a comparatively high 61.3%.

Stocks are scored on a set of parameters reflecting fundamental analytical tools involving valuation, size and financial performance. They are ranked according to the average values of those parameters. The highest ranking is 5 and the lowest ranking is 1.

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

SECTION 2 TODAY'S BULLISH SIGNALS

2.1 Uptrend

Today's Volatility:

The 4% discount to 12-month high of $134.49 against the 20% premium to 12-month low of $107.42 suggests the stock continues to push for new highs.

Today its volatility (highest price minus lowest price/lowest price) of 1.6% equalled the average daily volatility, up from 1.2584091808468567% on Wednesday. Price/Moving Average Price of 1.05:

- The Price/MAP 200 for Duke Energy Corporation is 1.05. Being higher than 1 is a bullish indicator. The stock is trading above both its MAPs and the 50-day MAP of $133.03 is higher than the 200-day MAP of $122.48, a second bullish indicator. The 200-day MAP has increased to $122.48, a third bullish indicator.

Past Quarter:

- In the last three months the stock has hit a new 52-week high twelve times, pointing to a significant uptrend.

2.2 Other Bullish Signals

- Return on Equity of 9.0% versus sector average of 5.8%.

MCap/Total Assets:

- Tobin's Q Ratio, defined as MCap divided by Total Assets, is 0.5. Compared with the rest of the market the stock is undervalued.

- Net profit margin has averaged 14.4% in the last 3 years. This is better than the sector average of 11.8% and suggests a high margin of safety.

- As per the Du Pont analysis, Return on Equity of 9.0% is better than sector average of 5.8%. This is computed as net profit margin of 15.2% times asset turnover [sales/assets] of 0.16 times leverage factor [total assets/shareholders' equity] of 3.6.
