Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

value.Title: Data centers can push Ohio green energy if lawmakers act, per report
value.Overview: The Columbus Dispatch, UTILITIES NEWS & ENVIRONMENT NEWS, 614words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJG-0H93-S10P-2533-00000-00Data centers can push Ohio green energy if lawmakers act, per report2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexisUTILITIES NEWS & ENVIRONMENT NEWSData centers can push Ohio green energy if lawmakers act, per reportDean Narciso, Columbus Dispatch

Critics of data centers point to their takeover of open land, drain on the power grid and excessive consumption of water. Those who live near them say they reduce property values, produce annoying sounds and occasionally malfunction, requiring emergency response.

But a group of environmentalists argued in a new report that data centers can help promote and expand clean energy, as long as they draw power from sustainable, environmentally friendly sources and not fossil fuels, especially natural gas.

"In the clean energy manufacturing sector, Ohio is already leading the field with two major solar manufacturers and significant investments in battery cell manufacturing. Thousands of Ohioans are employed in the growing carbon-free energy sector. In contrast, Ohio counties with the most fracking for fossil fuels have seen a loss in employment and population since the fracking boom began in 2008," according to Save Ohio Parks, a non-profit organization created in 2023 to protect parks, forests, and public lands from oil and gas drilling (fracking) and which promotes environmental preservation, public health, and cleaner energy.

The organization wants a moratorium on data center approvals until new regulations are adopted, including the banning of nondisclosure agreements. Data centers would be required to meet or offset their energy demand entirely with utility-scale solar, wind and energy storage sources.

In addition, companies would be required to pay for necessary power grid modernization costs to support gridreliability and to comply with water efficiency measures and public reporting on water use.

Ohio, with almost 12 million residents, is the nation's seventh most populous. Yet it has the fifth-most data centers with more than 200, more than half of them in central Ohio.

Residents of Hilliard, Sunbury, Pickaway County and elsewhere have objected to plans for further data center projects.

And statewide opposition to them is growing. In March, a statewide ballot initiate was submitted to ban data centers larger than 25 megawatts. The Ohio Ballot Board has approved the group to begin collecting signatures, with more 413,000 needed from half of Ohio’s 88 counties by July 1 to put the initiative on the November ballot, according to the report.

Ohio already is attractive to data center investors with flat, undeveloped land; electric capacity andrelatively low cost real estate and labor, according to the report. Since 2013, Ohio has offered a sales tax exemption for data center equipment, which has helped centers proliferate, the study says.

Link to Image

And the federal government is pushing for gas-fueled power plants to support them, calling U.S. leadership a geopolitical necessity in order to compete for jobs, artificial intelligence and AI-powered military warfare.

One of the nation's largest proposed centers would be in Pike County, relying on a proposed $33 billion natural gas power plant producing roughly 9.2 gigawatts, enough to power half of Ohio's electricity.

The reports recommends continuing the tax breaks contingent upon data centers establishing energy and waterefficiency measures; reporting publicly on their energy and water use; or subsidizing residential energy efficiency upgrades including rooftop solar, and energy storage.

The report's release coincides with a news conference in Hilliard to discuss how Ohio can leverage the data center boom to expand clean energy rather than accelerate fracking on public lands.

Growth and development reporter Dean Narciso can be reached at dnarciso@dispatch.com .

This article originally appeared on The Columbus Dispatch: Data centers can push Ohio green energy if lawmakers act, per report

urn:contentItem:6JJP-B1Y3-RWVF-P2RB-00000-00[Event] Kentucky New Energy Summit - June 9th, Lexington, KY2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexis[Event] Kentucky New Energy Summit - June 9th, Lexington, KY

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Bricker Graydon Wyatt LLP

May 12th, 2026 ( JD Supra - Delivered by Newstex )

June 9th, 2026

10:00 AM EDT

Marriott Lexington Griffin Gate Golf Resort & Spa

1800 Newtown Pike

Lexington, KY 40511

The Mid-Atlantic Renewable Energy Coalition (MAREC Action) and Bricker Graydon Wyatt invite you to join us for our 2nd annual summit exploring the future energy policy, development, and generation in Kentucky. Building on the success of last year's inaugural event, the summit will examine how an 'all of the above' energy strategy can drive economic development across the Commonwealth.

Last year's program explored rising energy demand, a range of generation technologies, transmission planning, and key policy and financing considerations, alongside practical lessons from local leaders on siting and permitting clean energy projects. The event was highlighted by a keynote address from Rocky Adkins, who offered valuable insight on Kentucky's energy future, economic competitiveness, and policy leadership.

This day-long summit is designed for state and local government officials, economic development leaders, energy developers, financial experts in the energy sector, and large-scale energy users.

Link to the original story.

value.Title: Data centers can push Ohio green energy if lawmakers act, per report
value.Overview: The Repository Online (Ohio), UTILITIES NEWS & ENVIRONMENT NEWS, 654words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-YRB3-RXK8-C1KM-00000-00Data centers can push Ohio green energy if lawmakers act, per report2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:24ZLexisNexisUTILITIES NEWS & ENVIRONMENT NEWSData centers can push Ohio green energy if lawmakers act, per reportDean Narciso, Columbus Dispatch

Critics of data centers point to their takeover of open land, drain on the power grid and excessive consumption of water. Those who live near them say they reduce property values, produce annoying sounds and occasionally malfunction, requiring emergency response.

But a group of environmentalists argued in a new report that data centers can help promote and expand clean energy, as long as they draw power from sustainable, environmentally friendly sources and not fossil fuels, especially natural gas.

"In the clean energy manufacturing sector, Ohio is already leading the field with two major solar manufacturers and significant investments in battery cell manufacturing. Thousands of Ohioans are employed in the growing carbon-free energy sector. In contrast, Ohio counties with the most fracking for fossil fuels have seen a loss in employment and population since the fracking boom began in 2008," according to Save Ohio Parks, a nonprofit organization created in 2023 to protect parks, forests, and public lands from oil and gas drilling and which promotes environmental preservation, public health, and cleaner energy.

The organization wants a moratorium on data center approvals until new regulations are adopted, including the banning of nondisclosure agreements. Data centers would be required to meet or offset their energy demand entirely with utility-scale solar, wind and energy storage sources.

In addition, companies would be required to pay for necessary power grid modernization costs to support grid reliability and to comply with water efficiency measures and public reporting on water use.

Ohio, with almost 12 million residents, is the nation's seventh most populous. Yet it has the fifth-most data centers with more than 200, more than half of them in central Ohio.

Residents in Perry Township, Canton, Hilliard, Sunbury, Pickaway County and other communities around the state have objected to plans for further data center projects. A data center planned for Faircrest Street SW and Kropf Avenue SW in Perry Township — which government officials have said will be an Amazon facility — has drawn criticism from area residents.

And statewide opposition to them is growing. In March, a statewide ballot initiate was submitted to ban data centers larger than 25 megawatts. The Ohio Ballot Board has approved the group to begin collecting signatures, with more 413,000 needed from half of Ohio’s 88 counties by July 1 to put the initiative on the November ballot, according to the report.

Ohio already is attractive to data center investors with flat, undeveloped land; electric capacity andrelatively low cost real estate and labor, according to the report. Since 2013, Ohio has offered a sales tax exemption for data center equipment, which has helped centers proliferate, the study says.

Link to Image

And the federal government is pushing for gas-fueled power plants to support them, calling U.S. leadership a geopolitical necessity in order to compete for jobs, artificial intelligence and AI-powered military warfare.

One of the nation's largest proposed centers would be in Pike County, relying on a proposed $33 billion natural gas power plant producing roughly 9.2 gigawatts, enough to power half of Ohio's electricity.

The Save Ohio Parks report recommends continuing the tax breaks contingent upon data centers establishing energy and water efficiency measures; reporting publicly on their energy and water use; or subsidizing residential energy efficiency upgrades, including rooftop solar, and energy storage.

The report's release coincides with a news conference in Hilliard to discuss how Ohio can leverage the data center boom to expand clean energy rather than accelerate fracking on public lands.

Growth and development reporter Dean Narciso can be reached at dnarciso@dispatch.com .

This article originally appeared on The Repository: Data centers can push Ohio green energy if lawmakers act, per report
